children.title: Glaucoma
Optic Nerve Anatomy
Optic nerve head (ONH) = optic disc; ~1.5mm diameter
Cup-to-disc ratio (C/D): normal ≤0.5; asymmetry >0.2 = suspicious
Neuroretinal rim: ISNT rule (Inferior > Superior > Nasal > Temporal thickness)
Retinal nerve fiber layer (RNFL): thickest superiorly and inferiorly (arcuate bundles)
Lamina cribrosa: sieve-like scleral plate where axons exit; vulnerable to IOP-induced damage
4 regions of optic nerve: intraocular (1mm), intraorbital (25mm), intracanalicular (9mm), intracranial (10mm)
Blood Supply to the Optic Nerve
Optic disc: short posterior ciliary arteries (SPCAs) → circle of Zinn-Haller
Prelaminar & laminar regions: branches of SPCAs
Retrolaminar ON: pial arterial plexus from ophthalmic artery
Central retinal artery supplies surface nerve fiber layer only
Vascular insufficiency (ischemia) = key mechanism in NTG and NAION
Pathophysiology
Definition: optic neuropathy with characteristic structural damage + corresponding visual field loss
IOP-dependent mechanism: elevated IOP → mechanical compression at lamina cribrosa → axon loss
IOP-independent mechanism: vascular insufficiency, glutamate excitotoxicity, oxidative stress, neuroinflammation
Retinal ganglion cells (RGCs) are the primary cells lost; irreversible
RNFL thinning precedes detectable visual field loss (structural before functional)
Target IOP: individualized based on baseline, progression rate, life expectancy
Classification
Open Angle
Angle open on gonioscopy; TM accessible
Increased resistance at JCT (juxtacanalicular TM) → ↑IOP
Includes: POAG, NTG, secondary open-angle types
Angle Closure
Iris physically obstructs TM → ↑IOP
Mechanisms: pupillary block (most common), plateau iris, lens-induced
Spectrum: PACS → PAC → PACG
Combined Mechanism
Both trabecular damage AND angle closure contribute
Example: chronic angle closure with synechiae + concurrent TM dysfunction
Epidemiology
Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma (POAG)
Most common glaucoma worldwide; leading cause of irreversible blindness
Prevalence ~2-3% in adults >40; ↑with age
Higher prevalence and severity in African and Hispanic populations
Risk factors: elevated IOP, age, family history, African ancestry, myopia, thin CCT, large C/D ratio
Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma (PACG)
More prevalent in Asian populations (especially East and Southeast Asian)
Accounts for ~50% of glaucoma blindness globally despite lower prevalence
Risk factors: female sex, hyperopia, shallow anterior chamber, older age
Genetics
Juvenile Open-Angle Glaucoma (JOAG)
MYOC gene (myocilin/TIGR); autosomal dominant
Onset age 3-35; markedly elevated IOP; may need early surgery
Familial Normal-Tension Glaucoma
OPTN gene (optineurin); autosomal dominant
NTG with strong family history; consider vascular workup
Primary Congenital Glaucoma
CYP1B1 gene; autosomal recessive
Isolated trabeculodysgenesis → impaired aqueous outflow
Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome
PITX2 or FOXC1 mutations; autosomal dominant
Anterior segment dysgenesis: posterior embryotoxon, iris abnormalities, glaucoma in 50%
Systemic: dental hypoplasia, facial anomalies, umbilical abnormalities
Aniridia
PAX6 mutation; autosomal dominant
Absent/rudimentary iris; glaucoma develops from angle dysgenesis
Wilms tumor risk (WAGR syndrome) if deletion of 11p13
Genome-Wide Association Studies (GWAS)
Loci identified: CDKN2B-AS1 (9p21), TMCO1, CAV1/2, SIX1/6, ABCA1
Most variants have small individual effect; polygenic risk scores in development
Pseudoexfoliation Syndrome
LOXL1 gene (most common genetic association in glaucoma)
Fibrillar exfoliative material deposits on lens, TM, iris
Strong risk for secondary open-angle glaucoma (PXG)
Genetic Testing: clinical utility limited currently; useful for congenital/juvenile forms; genetic counseling essential
Environmental & Other Risk Factors
IOP: most modifiable risk factor; each 1 mmHg reduction slows progression
CCT (central corneal thickness): thin CCT = independent risk factor for POAG (OHTS data)
Myopia: associated with POAG; tilted disc can mimic glaucomatous damage
Systemic: hypertension, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea (may contribute to NTG)
Ethnicity: African ancestry = higher risk/severity; Asian = higher angle-closure risk
IOP & Aqueous Humor Dynamics
Aqueous Humor Production
Site: non-pigmented ciliary epithelium (inner layer of ciliary body)
Mechanisms: active secretion (75-80%, Na/K-ATPase + carbonic anhydrase), ultrafiltration, diffusion
Rate: ~2-2.5 µL/min
Composition: low protein, high ascorbate, glutathione; provides nutrition to avascular lens and cornea
Suppression: beta blockers (↓cAMP), CAIs (↓bicarbonate secretion), alpha-2 agonists
Aqueous Humor Outflow
Conventional (Trabecular) Pathway — ~85-90%
Flow: TM → Schlemm canal → collector channels → aqueous veins → episcleral veins
TM layers: uveal → corneoscleral → juxtacanalicular (JCT = main resistance site)
Pressure-dependent; main site for IOP-lowering drugs (PGs, ROCK inhibitors, miotics)
Uveoscleral (Unconventional) Pathway — ~10-15%
Aqueous percolates through ciliary muscle interstitium → suprachoroidal space → sclera
Pressure-independent; enhanced by prostaglandin analogues
Aqueous Humor Dynamics Measurements
Aqueous production: fluorophotometry (non-invasive tracer method)
Outflow facility (C): tonography; normal ~0.22-0.28 µL/min/mmHg
Episcleral venous pressure (EVP): ~8-10 mmHg; sets lower limit of achievable IOP
Uveoscleral outflow (Fu): measured by fluorophotometry or mathematical modeling
Friedenwald equation: IOP = (F/C) + EVP
Intraocular Pressure
IOP Distribution & Glaucoma
Population mean: ~15-16 mmHg; normal range: 10-21 mmHg (statistical, not physiologic)
IOP >21 mmHg = ocular hypertension; NOT diagnostic of glaucoma
NTG: glaucoma with IOP consistently ≤21 mmHg
IOP is the only modifiable risk factor proven to slow glaucoma progression
IOP Variations
Diurnal variation: typically highest in morning (peak 8-10 AM); fluctuation >6 mmHg = significant
Postural: higher supine than sitting (~2-3 mmHg)
Exercise: transient ↓IOP with aerobic exercise
Valsalva: ↑IOP; relevant during breath-holding, coughing
CCT effect: thick cornea → falsely high GAT reading; thin cornea → falsely low
Tonometry
Goldmann Applanation Tonometry (GAT): gold standard; flattens 3.06mm diameter area
GAT formula: IOP = applied force / area (Imbert-Fick law)
Affected by CCT, corneal curvature, tear film (fluorescein needed)
Non-contact tonometry (air puff): screening only; less accurate
Perkins tonometer: portable version of GAT; useful in supine/pediatric patients
iCare rebound tonometer: no anesthesia needed; useful in children and home monitoring
Dynamic contour tonometry (Pascal): CCT-independent; measures ocular pulse amplitude
Tactile tension: digital palpation; only for gross assessment (e.g., very high or very low IOP)
Clinical Evaluation
Patient History
Chief complaint: usually asymptomatic until advanced; acute angle closure → pain, halos, nausea/vomiting
IOP history: prior measurements, steroid use, trauma, prior eye surgery
Family history: first-degree relative with glaucoma = 4-9× ↑risk
Systemic medications: steroids (topical/inhaled/systemic → IOP rise); anticholinergics (angle closure risk)
Systemic conditions: vasospasm (migraine, Raynaud), sleep apnea, cardiovascular disease → NTG risk
Quality of life: functional impact (driving, reading, mobility); essential for treatment decisions
Social determinants: access to medications, follow-up compliance, health literacy
Anterior Segment Evaluation
Refractive error: myopia (POAG risk), hyperopia (angle closure risk)
Pupillary function: RAPD suggests asymmetric ON damage
Slit-Lamp Biomicroscopy
Conjunctiva: bleb assessment (post-trabeculectomy), injection pattern
Episclera/sclera: dilated episcleral vessels → elevated EVP (carotid-cavernous fistula, Sturge-Weber)
Cornea: Krukenberg spindle (PDS), band keratopathy, corneal edema (acute angle closure)
Anterior chamber: Van Herick grading (depth estimation); cells/flare (uveitic glaucoma)
Iris: rubeosis (neovascular glaucoma), transillumination defects (PDS), ruff loss (PXE)
Lens: exfoliative material on anterior capsule (PXE), phacodonesis (lens instability), pseudophakodonesis
Gonioscopy
ESSENTIAL for all glaucoma patients; determines open vs closed angle
Direct gonioscopy (Koeppe lens): real angle view; used in OR, children
Indirect gonioscopy (Goldmann, Zeiss, Posner): mirrored view; most common clinical use
Angle structures (anterior to posterior): Schwalbe line → TM (pigmented) → scleral spur → ciliary band → iris
Shaffer grading: 0 (closed) → 1 (10°, high risk closure) → 2 (20°, possible closure) → 3 (30°, open) → 4 (40°, wide open)
Spaeth grading: more detailed; iris insertion level, angle width, iris contour
Peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS): angle closure evidence; document location and extent
Pigmentation: grade 0-4 (Scheie); heavy pigment in PDS, PXE; may indicate prior inflammation
Indentation gonioscopy (Zeiss/Posner): distinguishes appositional from synechial closure
Anterior Segment Imaging
UBM (Ultrasound Biomicroscopy): 50 MHz; images behind iris plane; plateau iris, ciliary body cysts, PCO
AS-OCT (Anterior Segment OCT): fast, non-contact; angle imaging, CCT, ACD; limited penetration behind iris
Specular Microscopy: endothelial cell count and morphology; pre-op assessment, ICE syndrome
Pachymetry: CCT measurement; Goldmann tonometry correction; OHTS risk stratification
Posterior Segment: Optic Nerve & RNFL
Optic Nerve Head Examination
Stereoscopic disc evaluation: essential (90D or 78D lens with slit lamp, or direct ophthalmoscope)
C/D ratio: vertical C/D more important; asymmetry between eyes >0.2 = suspicious
Neuroretinal rim: thinning/notching (especially inferior > superior) = classic glaucomatous change
ISNT rule violation: Inferior or Superior thinner than Temporal = abnormal
Disc hemorrhage (Drance hemorrhage): splinter at disc margin; associated with NTG; predicts progression
Parapapillary atrophy: beta-zone (Bruch membrane loss) = more significant; alpha-zone = normal aging
Laminar dot sign: visible lamina cribrosa pores in deep cupping
RNFL Evaluation
Slit-lamp RNFL assessment: bright striations in arcuate bundles (best seen with red-free light)
RNFL defects: wedge defects correspond to scotomas; usually superior/inferior arcuate regions
RNFL loss precedes visual field loss by years
OCT Imaging
RNFL thickness map: peripapillary RNFL; compares to normative database; inferior/superior thinning most sensitive
Optic nerve head analysis: C/D, rim area, disc area, cup volume
Macular ganglion cell layer (GCL+): highly sensitive for early glaucoma; inner inferior macula most vulnerable
Progression detection: event-based (TSNIT deviation from baseline) vs trend-based (linear regression over time)
Image quality: signal strength >6 (Cirrus); poor quality = segmentation error → false positives
Lamina cribrosa OCT: prelaminar depth, curvature; structural marker for IOP-related damage
OCT-A (Angiography): measures peripapillary flow index; reduced in glaucoma; emerging biomarker
Perimetry (Visual Field Testing)
Basics
Standard Automated Perimetry (SAP): Humphrey Field Analyzer most common
Most common programs: 24-2 (54 test points), 10-2 (central 10°, 68 points), 30-2
SITA strategies: SITA Standard, SITA Fast, SITA Faster — threshold estimation with adaptive testing
Reliability Indices
Fixation losses: >20% = unreliable
False positives: >15% = trigger happy patient (overestimates sensitivity)
False negatives: >33% = fatigue or advanced disease
Gaze tracking: most modern HFA; provides fixation stability data
Key Indices
MD (Mean Deviation): overall field depression; negative = worse; <-2 dB = outside normal range
PSD (Pattern Standard Deviation): localized defects; elevated in focal glaucoma damage
GHT (Glaucoma Hemifield Test): compares superior vs inferior hemifields; Outside Normal Limits = significant
VFI (Visual Field Index): 0-100%; 100% = normal; <70% = advanced
Patterns of VF Loss in Glaucoma
Nasal step: asymmetry across horizontal meridian in nasal field
Arcuate (Bjerrum) scotoma: follows arcuate nerve fiber bundle; superior or inferior
Paracentral scotoma: within 10° of fixation; often early sign in NTG
Temporal wedge: nasal RNFL damage → temporal field defect
Advanced: altitudinal defect, tubular vision, central island loss (fixation affected late)
Progression Analysis
Event-based: GCP (Guided Progression Analysis) — flags points consistently worse than baseline
Trend-based: MD slope (dB/year); >-1 dB/year = rapid progression
Minimum 5 fields over 2 years needed for reliable progression detection
Frequency: every 6 months for new/unstable patients; annually when stable
Structure-Function Relationship
RNFL loss detectable before VF loss (structural precedes functional)
Floor effect: RNFL cannot thin below ~40-50 µm; beyond this, VF continues to deteriorate
Combining OCT + VF improves detection sensitivity and progression monitoring
Primary Open-Angle Glaucoma (POAG)
Clinical Features
Asymptomatic until advanced; bilateral (often asymmetric)
Open angle on gonioscopy; IOP usually >21 mmHg but not required
Characteristic optic nerve changes: cupping, rim thinning/notching, RNFL defects
Corresponding VF defects
Atypical Findings
IOP in normal range (→ consider NTG)
Large physiologic cup (family history; compare disc photos)
Disc drusen: can mimic RNFL loss; evaluate with B-scan, autofluorescence
Risk Factors
Elevated IOP: strongest modifiable risk factor
Age: prevalence doubles each decade after 40
Race: African Americans → 4-5× more prevalent; earlier onset; more severe
Family history: first-degree relative → significantly higher risk
Thin CCT: <555 µm = highest risk category (OHTS)
Large vertical C/D ratio: ≥0.6 suspicious; ≥0.8 high risk
Myopia: ≥-3D associated with POAG; altered laminar biomechanics
Disc hemorrhage: strong predictor of progression, especially NTG
Association With Systemic Conditions
Diabetes: ↑POAG risk; may affect RGC or optic nerve blood flow
Hypothyroidism: associated with glaucoma
Sleep apnea: ↑risk NTG; nocturnal hypoxemia → optic nerve ischemia
Migraines/vasospasm: associated with NTG
Hypertension: complex relationship; both high and low (especially nocturnal dipping) can worsen NTG
Prognosis & Therapy
Goal: preserve vision and quality of life; slow/halt structural and functional progression
Target IOP: typically 20-30% reduction from baseline; lower for advanced/rapid progression
EMGT: showed 25% IOP reduction delayed progression by median 2 years
CIGT: laser trabeculoplasty as effective as medications initially (LiGHT trial: SLT first-line)
Glaucoma Suspect
Ocular Hypertension (OHT): IOP >21 mmHg with normal disc and VF; OHTS: 5-year conversion rate ~10% without treatment
Suspicious disc: large C/D, rim thinning, disc hemorrhage — but normal VF
VF defect without disc changes: scotoma present but disc looks normal
Management: close monitoring (OCT, VF every 6-12 months), treat if high risk
Normal-Tension Glaucoma (NTG)
Glaucomatous optic neuropathy with IOP consistently ≤21 mmHg
Risk factors: female sex, family history, OPTN/TBK1 mutations, vasospasm, sleep apnea, Japanese ancestry
More paracentral scotomas; more disc hemorrhages; greater macular RNFL involvement
Differential Diagnosis
Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION): pallid edema, altitudinal VF loss, acute onset
Compressive optic neuropathy: chiasmal lesion, pituitary tumor
Hereditary optic neuropathies: LHON (young male, cecocentral scotoma)
Optic neuritis: pain with eye movement, young female, MRI enhancement
Workup: 24-hour IOP phasing, CCT, MRI brain/orbits, blood pressure monitoring (nocturnal dipping)
Therapy: IOP reduction effective (CNTGS: 30% reduction slowed progression); also treat systemic vascular risk
Secondary Open-Angle Glaucoma
Pseudoexfoliation Syndrome (PXE/PXG)
Fibrillar exfoliative material from epithelial cells; deposits on lens, TM, iris
LOXL1 gene mutations; most common identifiable cause of secondary OAG worldwide
Signs: dandruff-like material on anterior lens capsule (central disc + peripheral ring), pupil margin, iris transillumination, Sampaolesi line (pigment on Schwalbe line)
IOP often markedly elevated and labile; significant diurnal variation
Poor pupil dilation; weak zonules → cataract surgery complications (nucleus drop)
Treatment: same as POAG; often requires surgery earlier; poor medication compliance worsens outcome
Pigment Dispersion Syndrome (PDS) / Pigmentary Glaucoma
Young myopic males; bilateral
Mechanism: iris bowing posteriorly → rubs against zonules → pigment liberation → blocks TM
Signs: Krukenberg spindle (vertical corneal endothelial pigment), iris transillumination defects (radial midperipheral), dense TM pigmentation
IOP spikes after exercise or pupil dilation
Treatment: miosis (pilocarpine prevents posterior bowing), laser iridotomy controversial, IOP lowering meds
Lens-Induced Glaucoma
Phacolytic: hypermature cataract leaks proteins → macrophages clog TM
Phacoantigenic (phacoanaphylactic): lens protein exposure → immune-mediated TM inflammation
Phacomorphic: intumescent lens → pushes iris forward → angle closure (secondary angle closure type)
Treatment: remove lens
Glaucoma from Intraocular Tumors
Melanoma: angle invasion, neovascularization, tumor cells in TM
Melanocytomalytic glaucoma: benign melanocytoma disperses pigment → TM block
Metastatic tumors: angle or ciliary body infiltration
Retinoblastoma: neovascular glaucoma in advanced cases
Ocular Inflammation (Uveitic Glaucoma)
Mechanisms: inflammatory cells/debris in TM, TM dysfunction from cytokines, steroid-induced, PAS formation
Fuchs heterochromic iridocyclitis: heterochromia, fine KPs, no PAS, mild glaucoma
HLA-B27 associated (ankylosing spondylitis, Reiter): aggressive anterior uveitis
Posner-Schlossman syndrome: acute recurrent unilateral elevated IOP with minimal cell; may be herpetic
Treatment: control inflammation first; steroids (beware IOP rise); miotics may help; trabeculectomy with antifibrotics if needed
Elevated Episcleral Venous Pressure (EVP)
EVP sets the lower limit of IOP (Friedenwald: IOP = F/C + EVP)
Causes: carotid-cavernous fistula (CCF), dural sinus fistula, Sturge-Weber syndrome, orbital varix, superior vena cava obstruction, thyroid ophthalmopathy
Signs: dilated tortuous episcleral vessels; blood in Schlemm canal on gonioscopy
Medical treatment limited (IOP won't go below elevated EVP); surgical outflow procedures needed
Trauma & Surgery-Related Glaucoma
Hyphema: RBC obstruction of TM; sickle cell patients highly sensitive to IOP elevation
Angle recession: tear between longitudinal and circular ciliary muscle; risk of late glaucoma (years to decades)
Ghost cell glaucoma: degenerated RBCs (ghost cells) from old vitreous hemorrhage obstruct TM
Post-surgical: aphakic/pseudophakic glaucoma; silicone oil in TM; epithelial downgrowth
Steroid-Induced Glaucoma
Steroids inhibit phagocytosis of ECM in TM → glycosaminoglycan accumulation → ↑resistance
~30% of population are 'steroid responders' (IOP rise >10 mmHg)
Risk: topical > periocular > systemic > inhaled; potency matters (dexamethasone > loteprednol)
POAG patients and relatives: highest steroid response risk
Treatment: stop steroid; switch to NSAIDs or loteprednol; IOP usually normalizes in weeks
Schwartz-Matsuo Syndrome
Rhegmatogenous retinal detachment → photoreceptor outer segment debris in AC → TM obstruction
Elevated IOP + RD; IOP normalizes after RD repair
Drugs & Glaucoma
Angle closure induction: anticholinergics, adrenergics, sulfonamides (topiramate: bilateral acute myopia + angle closure)
Open-angle IOP elevation: steroids, succinylcholine, ketamine
Primary Angle-Closure Disease
Pathogenesis
Pupillary Block (Most Common Mechanism)
Relative resistance to aqueous flow from posterior to anterior chamber at the pupillary margin
Pressure builds in posterior chamber → bows iris forward (iris bombé) → apposes iris to TM
Mid-dilation (3-5mm pupil) = highest risk (maximum relative block)
Plateau Iris
Anteriorly positioned ciliary processes push peripheral iris into angle (without pupillary block)
Iridotomy does NOT prevent closure; requires iridoplasty or lens extraction
Diagnosis confirmed by gonioscopy (double hump sign) or UBM
Medications That Can Trigger Angle Closure
Mydriatics: tropicamide, phenylephrine → pupil dilation in predisposed eye
Topiramate: idiosyncratic bilateral acute angle closure + myopia; mechanism = suprachoroidal effusion
Sulfonamides, promethazine, SSRIs: reported cases of acute angle closure
Risk Factors
Asian ethnicity: especially East/South Asian (smaller eyes, shallower angles)
Female sex: 2-4× more common
Hyperopia: shorter axial length → smaller anterior segment
Increasing age: lens thickens and moves anteriorly; iris-lens contact increases
Shallow ACD (<2.5mm): strong independent risk factor
Family history: first-degree relative with PACG
Provocative factors: dim lighting, emotional stress, prolonged reading (near vision → miosis then pupil fatigue)
PACD Spectrum
Primary Angle-Closure Suspect (PACS)
Iridotrabecular contact (ITC) in ≥2 quadrants; no peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS); normal IOP; normal disc
Management: observation vs prophylactic iridotomy (debated; ZAP trial: LPI prevented significant events)
Primary Angle Closure (PAC)
ITC + PAS or elevated IOP; no glaucomatous optic neuropathy
Treat with laser peripheral iridotomy (LPI)
Primary Angle-Closure Glaucoma (PACG)
PAC + glaucomatous optic neuropathy ± VF defect
More aggressive treatment: LPI + IOP-lowering meds; consider lens extraction
Symptomatic (Acute) Angle Closure
Severe eye pain, headache, nausea/vomiting, halos around lights, blurred vision
Signs: conjunctival injection, corneal edema, mid-dilated fixed pupil, very elevated IOP (often >40-50 mmHg), shallow AC
Emergency treatment: systemic CAI (acetazolamide 500mg IV/PO), topical beta blocker, alpha agonist, pilocarpine 1-2%; IV mannitol if needed
Definitive: LPI after IOP control; fellow eye prophylactic LPI
Plateau Iris Management
LPI first (to eliminate pupillary block component)
If angle still closes (confirmed on gonioscopy post-LPI): laser iridoplasty (ALPI)
Long-term pilocarpine: keeps iris pulled away from angle
Lens extraction: increasingly preferred (removes both pupillary block and pushes iris away)
Secondary Angle-Closure Glaucoma
With Pupillary Block
Phacomorphic: large intumescent lens → pupillary block → treat with lens extraction
Ectopia lentis: subluxed lens blocks pupil; treat with surgical lens removal
Aphakic pupillary block: vitreous face blocks pupil post-cataract surgery (no IOL); treat with LPI
Posterior synechiae (360° seclusio pupillae): uveitis causes iris bombé; treat underlying inflammation + LPI
Without Pupillary Block — Pulling Mechanism
Neovascular glaucoma (NVG): rubeosis iridis → fibrovascular membrane contracts → closes angle; causes: PDR, CRVO, ocular ischemic syndrome
ICE syndrome: endothelial proliferation → PAS, angle closure; unilateral; Chandler, Cogan-Reese, essential iris atrophy
Epithelial/fibrous ingrowth: post-surgical; membrane over angle
Without Pupillary Block — Pushing Mechanism
Malignant glaucoma (aqueous misdirection): aqueous enters vitreous → posterior push → entire lens-iris diaphragm forward; treat with cycloplegics, vitrectomy
Suprachoroidal hemorrhage: pushes angle forward; post-surgical or spontaneous
Uveal effusion/ciliochoroidal detachment: topiramate, posterior scleritis, nanophthalmos
Retinopathy of prematurity, persistent fetal vasculature: retrolental mass pushes iris-lens forward
Neovascular Glaucoma (NVG) — High Yield
Etiology: ischemia → VEGF ↑ → rubeosis iridis → fibrovascular membrane over angle
Stages: rubeosis only → open angle (early NVG) → closed angle (late NVG)
Causes: PDR (most common), CRVO, ocular ischemic syndrome, carotid artery disease
Treatment: anti-VEGF (intravitreal) first → pan-retinal photocoagulation → glaucoma surgery (tube shunt preferred over trab)
Glaucoma in Children & Adolescents
Classification
Primary congenital glaucoma (PCG): isolated trabeculodysgenesis; birth to age 3
Juvenile open-angle glaucoma (JOAG): age 3-35; MYOC gene
Secondary glaucoma with anterior segment dysgenesis: Peters, Axenfeld-Rieger, Aniridia
Secondary glaucoma without dysgenesis: Sturge-Weber, trauma, uveitis
Primary Congenital Glaucoma (PCG)
Incidence: ~1:10,000 births; bilateral in 70%; sporadic or AR (CYP1B1)
Pathophysiology: abnormal development of TM → ↑outflow resistance → ↑IOP → globe enlargement (buphthalmos)
Classic Triad
Epiphora (tearing)
Photophobia
Blepharospasm
Signs: buphthalmos (enlarged cornea >12mm in infant), Haab striae (horizontal corneal breaks from Descemet), corneal edema, increased C/D
Differential: nasolacrimal duct obstruction (no IOP elevation, no corneal changes), megalocornea, birth trauma
Treatment: SURGICAL first-line (not medical); goniotomy or trabeculotomy
Goniotomy: direct angle incision under direct visualization; requires clear cornea
Trabeculotomy: external approach; works with cloudy cornea
Prognosis: if treated early, ~80% achieve IOP control; amblyopia management essential
Secondary Glaucomas
Axenfeld-Rieger Syndrome
PITX2 or FOXC1; AD; spectrum of anterior segment dysgenesis
Posterior embryotoxon (prominent Schwalbe line), iris strands to Schwalbe line, iris holes
Glaucoma in 50%; systemic: dental, facial, umbilical anomalies
Peters Anomaly
Central corneal opacity + absence of Descemet/endothelium + iridocorneal or lens-corneal adhesions
Type I: corneoiridic adhesions; Type II: also lens adherent to cornea
Glaucoma in ~50-70%; poor visual prognosis; consider corneal transplant
Aniridia
PAX6 mutation; autosomal dominant (sporadic cases: Wilms tumor risk → check WAGR)
Rudimentary iris stump; associated: nystagmus, foveal hypoplasia, cataract, corneal pannus (aniridia-related keratopathy)
Glaucoma develops from progressive angle closure by iris stump; treat with surgery (goniotomy poor results → tube shunt)
Sturge-Weber Syndrome
Port-wine stain (facial nevus flammeus) → ipsilateral glaucoma (elevated EVP + possibly dysgenetic angle)
Glaucoma in ~30%; early onset → goniotomy/trabeculotomy; late onset → filtering surgery (beware suprachoroidal hemorrhage risk)
No gene identified; sporadic GNAQ somatic mutation
Pediatric Glaucoma Evaluation
Exam under anesthesia (EUA): IOP (note anesthesia effect; ketamine raises IOP; halogenated agents lower it), corneal diameter, pachymetry, gonioscopy, disc evaluation
Cycloplegic refraction: detect amblyogenic refractive error (myopia in PCG, hyperopia in some secondary glaucomas)
B-scan ultrasonography: vitreous hemorrhage, retinal detachment
A-scan: axial length monitoring (globe enlargement = poor IOP control)
Pediatric Glaucoma Treatment
Surgical first-line for PCG; medical adjunct post-surgery or for older children
Surgical options: goniotomy, trabeculotomy, combined trabeculotomy-trabeculectomy, tube shunts (Baerveldt, Ahmed)
Medical options: beta blockers (caution in infants: apnea, bradycardia), CAI drops, brimonidine (AVOID <2 years: CNS depression), PGA (some data)
Amblyopia treatment: patching, glasses — essential for visual development
Prognosis: PCG with early surgery ~80% success; secondary glaucomas generally worse prognosis
Medical Management of Glaucoma
Prostaglandin Analogues (PGAs) — 1st Line
Agents: latanoprost (Xalatan), bimatoprost (Lumigan), travoprost (Travatan Z), tafluprost (preservative-free)
Mechanism: ↑uveoscleral (unconventional) outflow via FP receptor; also ↑trabecular outflow
IOP reduction: 25-35% (most effective class)
Dosing: once daily (evening preferred)

Side effects: iris/periocular skin darkening (prostaglandin pigmentation), eyelash growth (hypertrichosis), periorbital fat atrophy (DUES = deepening upper eyelid sulcus), conjunctival hyperemia, CME (aphakic/pseudophakic), reactivation of herpes
Sustained-release bimatoprost implant (Durysta): biodegradable intracameral implant; single injection; ~4 months IOP reduction
Beta Blockers
Agents: timolol (non-selective β1+β2), betaxolol (selective β1)
Mechanism: ↓aqueous production by blocking β-adrenergic receptors on ciliary epithelium
IOP reduction: 20-27%
Dosing: BID (timolol); QD gel-forming solution
Contraindications: asthma, COPD, bradycardia, heart block, decompensated heart failure
Betaxolol: safer in reactive airway; slightly less effective; possible neuroprotective effect
Long-term drift: IOP lowering diminishes over time (tachyphylaxis)
Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors (CAIs)
Topical: dorzolamide (Trusopt) BID-TID; brinzolamide (Azopt) BID-TID
Systemic: acetazolamide (Diamox) 250-500mg; dichlorphenamide; methazolamide
Mechanism: inhibit CA-II in ciliary epithelium → ↓bicarbonate → ↓aqueous secretion
IOP reduction: 15-20% (topical); 25-30% (systemic)
Side effects (topical): stinging, altered taste, corneal decompensation (pre-existing endothelial disease)
Side effects (systemic): metabolic acidosis, renal stones, aplastic anemia (rare), paresthesias, malaise, sulfa allergy (cross-reactivity)
Contraindications: sulfa allergy; systemic CAI contraindicated in renal failure, hepatic failure
Alpha-2 Agonists
Agents: brimonidine (Alphagan P 0.1%) BID-TID; apraclonidine (Iopidine, short-term only)
Mechanism: ↓aqueous production + ↑uveoscleral outflow; also possible neuroprotective effect
IOP reduction: 18-27%
Side effects: ocular allergy (up to 25% with brimonidine), dry mouth, fatigue, CNS depression (contraindicated in children <2 years)
Apraclonidine: short-term use only (high allergy rate); useful perioperatively to prevent IOP spikes
Rho Kinase (ROCK) Inhibitors
Agent: netarsudil (Rhopressa) QD; also in combo with latanoprost (Rocklatan)
Mechanism: ↑trabecular outflow (relaxes TM cells), ↓aqueous production, ↓EVP
IOP reduction: ~20%; most effective at lower baseline IOPs
Side effects: conjunctival hyperemia (most common), cornea verticillata (benign, reversible), subconjunctival hemorrhage
Nitric Oxide Donors
Agent: latanoprostene bunod (Vyzulta) QD
Mechanism: PGA component (uveoscleral outflow) + NO release → ↑trabecular outflow via cGMP pathway
IOP reduction: slightly superior to latanoprost alone (VOYAGER trial)
Parasympathomimetics (Miotics)
Agent: pilocarpine 1-4% QID; carbachol
Mechanism: ciliary muscle contraction → opens TM → ↑trabecular outflow; also closes angle by pulling iris root
IOP reduction: 20-25%
Side effects: miosis (poor night vision), induced myopia, brow ache, retinal detachment risk (myopes), cataract (nuclear), rare: retinal detachment
Role: now mostly for angle closure (acute attack), pilocarpine insert for presbyopia
Hyperosmotic Agents
Agents: mannitol 20% IV (1-2 g/kg); glycerin 50% oral
Mechanism: draws fluid from vitreous (osmotic gradient) → rapidly lowers IOP
Use: acute IOP elevation (angle closure attack, before surgery)
Side effects: headache, fluid overload, CHF risk; glycerin metabolized → avoid in DM
Combination Medications
Cosopt: dorzolamide + timolol BID
Combigan: brimonidine + timolol BID
Simbrinza: brimonidine + brinzolamide BID-TID (beta-blocker free)
Rocklatan: latanoprost + netarsudil QD
Advantage: better adherence; fewer preservative doses; may have additive IOP reduction
General Approach to Medical Treatment
Start monotherapy (PGA 1st line); reassess at 4-6 weeks
Assess IOP at peak and trough; consider phasing for diurnal fluctuation
Add or switch if inadequate response; prefer fewer drops → better adherence
Benzalkonium chloride (BAC) preservative: toxic to ocular surface; consider preservative-free drops in dry eye patients
Pregnancy: CAIs and brimonidine relatively contraindicated; beta blockers relatively safer but monitor fetus; consult OB
Patient adherence: ~50% of patients non-adherent at 1 year; simplify regimen; use blister packs or apps; monitor at follow-up
Surgical Therapy for Glaucoma
Laser Trabeculoplasty
Types
ALT (Argon Laser Trabeculoplasty): 50µm spot, photocoagulates TM; limited retreatment
SLT (Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty): Q-switched Nd:YAG, 532nm; selectively targets pigmented TM cells; can be repeated
Mechanism: thermal/photochemical effect → ↑TM cell activity → improved outflow; also cytokine-mediated remodeling
IOP reduction: ~20-30%; comparable to PGA (LiGHT trial: SLT equivalent to drops as initial therapy)
Indications: newly diagnosed POAG/OHT (1st line per LiGHT), adjunct therapy, medication intolerance
Contraindications: uveitic glaucoma, NVG with rubeosis, angle closure (inadequate angle view)
Technique: 360° or 180° TM; 100 non-overlapping spots; energy titrated to champagne bubbling
Post-op: IOP spike first few hours (pre-treat with apraclonidine); monitor at 1 hour
Complications: transient IOP spike, mild inflammation, failure over time
Cyclophotocoagulation (CPC)
Mechanism: destroys ciliary epithelium → ↓aqueous production
Modes: trans-scleral (TSCPC) using diode laser; endoscopic (ECP) under direct visualization; micropulse CPC (titrated, less destructive)
Indications: refractory glaucoma, painful blind eye, poor surgical candidate
Complications: hypotony, phthisis bulbi, inflammation, sympathetic ophthalmia (rare), vision loss
Micropulse: more titrated; lower risk profile; increasing use earlier in disease
Laser Peripheral Iridotomy (LPI)
Creates opening in peripheral iris → equalizes pressure between posterior and anterior chambers → opens angle
Indications: PACS (controversial), PAC, PACG, acute angle closure (after IOP control), prophylaxis of fellow eye
Technique: Nd:YAG laser; superior iris 11-1 o'clock position (under lid); penetrate full thickness
Post-op: apraclonidine 1 hour prior; steroid drops post-procedure
Complications: transient IOP spike, hyphema, iritis, dysphotopsia (if not under lid)
Efficacy: very effective for pupillary block; does NOT help plateau iris (need ALPI or lens extraction)
Laser Iridoplasty (ALPI)
Argon laser applied to peripheral iris → contracts stroma → pulls iris from angle
Indications: plateau iris syndrome (after LPI), acute angle closure when LPI cannot be done (corneal edema)
Technique: 500µm spot, low power, long duration; peripheral iris, 24-32 spots per 360°
Trabeculectomy
Gold standard filtering surgery; creates fistula from anterior chamber to subconjunctival space (bleb)
IOP reduction: 30-50%; target IOP achievable in low teens
Antifibrotic agents: MMC (mitomycin C) most effective; 5-FU (5-fluorouracil); reduce bleb scarring
Indications: POAG/PACG not controlled medically; NTG requiring very low IOP; failed SLT
Contraindications: relative: active inflammation, conjunctival scarring, neovascularization
Post-op Complications
Early: shallow AC (wound leak, overfiltration, choroidal detachment), hyphema, infection
Late: bleb failure (encapsulation/scarring), blebitis, bleb-related endophthalmitis, hypotony maculopathy
Blebitis → endophthalmitis: aggressive treatment (intravitreal antibiotics)
Plate-Based Tube Shunt Surgery
Types: Ahmed valve (FP7, FP8 — valved), Baerveldt (250, 350mm² — non-valved), Molteno
Mechanism: tube from AC or PC → equatorial plate → aqueous drains to fibrous capsule → episcleral veins
IOP reduction: similar to trabeculectomy long-term (TVT study: equivalent at 3 years)
Indications: prior failed trab, conjunctival scarring, uveitic/NVG/silicone oil glaucoma, pediatric glaucoma
Ahmed: valved → lower risk of early hypotony; hypertensive phase (fibrosis 6-12 weeks) is common
Baerveldt: non-valved → rigate tube initially; deeper IOP reduction long-term
Complications
Tube-cornea touch → endothelial damage → corneal decompensation
Tube exposure → endophthalmitis
Diplopia from plate restricting EOM
Hypertensive phase: IOP spike at 4-8 weeks post Baerveldt (add meds temporarily)
Microinvasive Glaucoma Surgery (MIGS)
Characteristics
Ab interno approach; minimal tissue disruption; combined with cataract surgery or standalone
Safety profile superior to trab/tube; less IOP reduction
Types by Mechanism
Trabecular bypass: iStent inject W (2 stents, Schlemm canal), Hydrus microstent (18mm Schlemm canal scaffold)
Schlemm canal/TM ablation: Kahook dual blade (excises TM), Goniotomy, GATT (gonioscopy-assisted transluminal trabeculotomy)
Suprachoroidal: CyPass (withdrawn 2018 due to late endothelial loss); iStent Supra
Subconjunctival: XEN gel stent (gelatin, 45µm lumen) → bleb without conjunctival incision; Preserflo MicroShunt (SIBS polymer)
Best candidates: mild-moderate POAG + cataract; trabecular bypass best when angle open and pigmented
XEN/Preserflo: more IOP reduction; bleb-related complications still possible
Nonpenetrating Glaucoma Surgery
Deep sclerectomy: removes inner wall of Schlemm canal + TM outer wall; leaves trabeculo-Descemet membrane
Viscocanalostomy: dilates Schlemm canal with OVD
Lower bleb-related complication rate vs trabeculectomy; IOP reduction less predictable
Cataract Surgery in Glaucoma
Phacoemulsification lowers IOP ~2-4 mmHg; effect greatest in angle-closure glaucoma (opens angle)
Combined phaco + trabeculectomy (phacotrabeculectomy): lower IOP but more complications vs trabeculectomy alone
Combined phaco + MIGS: growing preference for mild-moderate glaucoma with cataract
Angle closure: lens extraction alone is highly effective (EAGLE trial: lens extraction > LPI for PAC/PACG)
Top 20 Glaucoma Exam Facts
Glaucoma = optic neuropathy with characteristic structural loss + VF defect (IOP alone is NOT diagnostic)
RNFL thinning precedes detectable VF loss (structural damage before functional)
JCT (juxtacanalicular TM) = main site of aqueous outflow resistance
Aqueous flow: ciliary epithelium → posterior chamber → pupil → anterior chamber → TM → Schlemm canal → episcleral veins
Goldmann tonometry (GAT): gold standard for IOP; thick CCT → falsely high reading; thin CCT → falsely low
Normal IOP range: 10-21 mmHg; NTG = glaucoma with IOP ≤21 mmHg
POAG risk factors: elevated IOP, thin CCT, large C/D, African ancestry, family history, myopia
PCG classic triad: epiphora + photophobia + blepharospasm; SURGICAL treatment first (goniotomy/trabeculotomy)
Angle closure emergency: severe pain + halos + N/V + mid-dilated fixed pupil + ↑IOP → treat with IV acetazolamide + mannitol + topical meds → definitive LPI
Pupillary block = most common mechanism of angle closure; treat with LPI
Plateau iris: LPI does NOT prevent closure; needs ALPI or lens extraction
NVG: rubeosis → fibrovascular membrane → angle closure; causes: PDR, CRVO, OIS; treat with anti-VEGF + PRP + tube shunt
LOXL1 gene: most common genetic association in glaucoma (Pseudoexfoliation)
PDS: Krukenberg spindle + iris transillumination defects (radial, mid-peripheral) + dense TM pigment; young myopic male
SLT: equivalent to drops as first-line therapy (LiGHT trial); can be repeated; 1st line option
Trabeculectomy: gold standard filtering surgery; MMC antifibrotic; risk of bleb-related endophthalmitis (bleb leaks!)
Tube shunts: preferred in uveitic, NVG, silicone oil glaucoma, prior failed trab; TVT: similar IOP reduction to trab
MIGS: trabecular bypass stents (iStent, Hydrus) combined with cataract surgery; safest, least IOP reduction
EAGLE trial: lens extraction superior to LPI for PAC and PACG → consider as first-line in angle closure
Disc hemorrhage (Drance): splinter hemorrhage at disc margin; strong predictor of glaucoma progression, especially NTG
